analysis:
Step 1 — Genuine near-term risk assessment:

CL-01 (ECDH-P256, urgency 10): Highest urgency and broken by Shor's algorithm. The critical compounding factor here is the device lifecycle: implants are in-body for 8-12 years, firmware updates require a clinic visit, and the patient data sensitivity window is 20 years — well beyond the 15-year CRQC horizon. This means a reactive migration is physically impossible; by the time a CRQC threat materialises, many currently-implanted devices will not yet have had a clinic visit. Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later is an active threat today for cardiac telemetry data. Urgency score 10 is fully warranted and the migration_timeline_years of 6 must be treated as a hard ceiling, not a comfortable target. There is no protocol-redesign blocker per se, but the physical update constraint makes this the most operationally constrained asset in the portfolio.

CL-02 (ECDSA-P256, urgency 10): Equally scored and equally broken. This asset protects firmware authenticity — the root-of-trust for the implant itself. A compromised firmware signing key would allow an attacker to push malicious firmware to a pacemaker, which is a patient-safety issue, not merely a data-confidentiality issue. The severity here arguably exceeds CL-01 because the attack surface is integrity/safety, not just confidentiality. Same physical update constraint applies. Critically, CL-02 cannot be migrated independently of CL-01 — both live in the same implant firmware and any firmware update that introduces ML-KEM-512 for key exchange must simultaneously update the signature verification logic. These two assets are tightly coupled and must be batched into a single firmware release.

CL-03 (AES-128-GCM, urgency 5): Weakened but not broken. The path to remediation is a key-size change to AES-256, not a PQC algorithm swap, making this the lowest-complexity firmware change of the three implant assets. However, because it lives in the same firmware as CL-01 and CL-02, the pragmatic approach is to bundle this change into the same firmware update release rather than consume a separate clinic-visit update cycle. Treating it as a standalone lower-priority item would be operationally wasteful given the update delivery mechanism. Its urgency score of 5 reflects the Grover threat being quadratic (not exponential) but the 20-year data sensitivity window and Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later exposure still make this a live concern.

CL-04 (RSA-2048, urgency 8): Broken by Shor's algorithm, located on a standard clinic gateway server with a migration_timeline_years of 2 and no firmware-delivery blockers. The only blocker is the data sensitivity window. This asset is materially easier to migrate than the implant assets — it is a conventional TLS certificate replacement on server infrastructure — and yet it is being scored here slightly lower than CL-01/CL-02 purely because the operational risk of delay is lower (a server cert can be rotated at any time without a patient clinic visit). However, the 2-year migration timeline means this is actually the most time-pressured asset in terms of 'when can work start and be completed', and it is the only asset that can be migrated immediately in Q1 without waiting for a firmware release cycle. Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later applies because the gateway handles the same 20-year-sensitive patient data.

Step 2 — Dependency mapping and batching:

Batch A (Implant firmware — CL-01, CL-02, CL-03): All three assets reside in the same implant firmware image. Any firmware update delivered via clinic visit should bundle all three changes: replace ECDH-P256 with hybrid X25519+ML-KEM-512 (CL-01), replace ECDSA-P256 root-of-trust verification with ML-DSA-44 plus SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s (CL-02), and upsize the AES key to 256-bit (CL-03). Splitting these across separate firmware releases would waste patient clinic visits, create a longer window of partial vulnerability, and add unnecessary regulatory re-certification cycles (FDA 510(k)/De Novo for each firmware revision). CL-02 is sequenced first within the batch because the signing key infrastructure (new ML-DSA-44 signing certificates, revocation of the ECDSA root) must be established on the clinic-side before a signed firmware update can be pushed to devices. CL-01 and CL-03 follow as co-delivered payload changes in the same firmware image.

Batch B (Gateway server — CL-04): CL-04 is independent of the implant firmware. It can and should be migrated immediately on standard server infrastructure timelines. It gates nothing in Batch A but also is not gated by Batch A. It has the shortest migration_timeline_years (2) and no firmware-delivery constraint, making it the natural Q1 action item.

Step 3 — Realistic Q1 migration plan:

Q1 is not the time to finish the implant firmware migration — that is a multi-year regulatory and engineering program. Q1 is the time to: (a) execute the fully unblocked action (CL-04 gateway TLS certificate rotation to ML-DSA-44), and (b) formally initiate the implant firmware program with the correct sequencing. Concretely:

Weeks 1-4: Begin CL-04 migration. Procure or generate ML-DSA-44 TLS certificates for the clinic gateway remote monitoring portal. Stage in parallel (not replacing) with existing RSA-2048 cert to validate compatibility with clinic-side TLS stacks and any patient-facing monitoring applications. Plan for dual-stack operation during transition.

Weeks 2-6: Initiate CL-02 key infrastructure groundwork. Generate new ML-DSA-44 and SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s firmware signing key pairs. Establish secure key ceremony procedures. This must precede any firmware build because the new signing keys need to be operationally ready before a firmware image can be signed and validated end-to-end. This is the critical-path predecessor for Batch A.

Weeks 4-8: Engage regulatory and clinical engineering teams to scope the combined CL-01 + CL-02 + CL-03 firmware update. A single combined firmware revision minimises 510(k) submission cycles. Begin threat modelling for the hybrid X25519+ML-KEM-512 key exchange and confirm resource constraints (RAM, flash, clock cycles) on the implant MCU for ML-KEM-512 and ML-DSA-44 operations — power and compute are non-trivial constraints in implantable devices.

End of Q1 deliverable: CL-04 fully migrated to ML-DSA-44 on the gateway (or at minimum dual-stack operational with RSA-2048 retirement date committed). CL-02 new signing key infrastructure generated and secured. Implant firmware migration program formally scoped with regulatory pathway confirmed. This positions the organisation to execute the firmware update in the next release window without losing a full quarter to planning overhead.

Priority ranking summary: CL-02 is ranked first because it is the root-of-trust safety-critical predecessor whose key infrastructure must be established before any firmware update can be signed and delivered — it gates the entire implant batch. CL-01 is ranked second as the primary confidentiality risk driver and equally urgent, co-delivered with CL-02 in the same firmware image. CL-04 is ranked third because it is the only asset executable immediately in Q1 with no dependencies and the shortest migration timeline. CL-03 is ranked fourth not because it is less important but because it is the lowest-complexity change, fully batched with CL-01/CL-02, and does not require independent action.

recommendations.rationale: ECDSA-P256 root-of-trust for firmware signing is a patient-safety risk, not merely a confidentiality risk — a compromised signing key enables malicious firmware on an implanted pacemaker. It is also the critical-path predecessor for the entire implant firmware batch: new ML-DSA-44 and SLH-DSA-SHA2-128s signing key infrastructure must be generated and operationally validated before any PQC firmware image can be built, signed, and delivered to devices. Q1 action is to initiate the key ceremony and establish the new signing infrastructure immediately, even though the firmware itself will take longer to certify and deploy. Ranked first over CL-01 because CL-01's firmware changes cannot be delivered without CL-02's signing infrastructure being in place first.

ECDH-P256 for telemetry session key establishment is broken by Shor's algorithm and the 20-year patient data sensitivity window makes Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later an active threat today. The 8-12 year device implant lifecycle means reactive migration after a CRQC emerges is physically infeasible for a large fraction of the installed device base — many patients will not have had a clinic visit by then. Migration to hybrid X25519+ML-KEM-512 must be co-delivered with CL-02 in a single firmware image to avoid wasting clinic visit update cycles and to minimise regulatory re-certification overhead. Q1 action is to begin MCU resource profiling (RAM, flash, power budget) for ML-KEM-512 and confirm feasibility within implant hardware constraints, in parallel with CL-02 key infrastructure work.

RSA-2048 on the clinic gateway TLS stack has the shortest migration_timeline_years (2), no firmware-delivery blocker, and no dependency on the implant batch. It is the only asset in the portfolio that can be fully executed in Q1 on standard server infrastructure timelines. Although its urgency score (8) is lower than CL-01/CL-02 (10), it is operationally the most immediately actionable item and its 2-year migration window makes deferral risky. Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later applies because the gateway handles the same long-sensitivity cardiac data. Q1 action: procure ML-DSA-44 TLS certificates, stage dual-stack operation alongside RSA-2048 to validate compatibility, and commit to a hard RSA-2048 retirement date within the 2-year window.

AES-128-GCM is weakened rather than broken, and the remediation (key-size increase to AES-256) is the lowest-complexity change in the portfolio — no PQC algorithm replacement required, no new library dependencies. It is ranked fourth not because the risk is negligible (the 20-year data sensitivity window and Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later exposure are real) but because the correct execution strategy is to bundle this change into the same firmware image as CL-01 and CL-02, consuming no additional clinic visit or regulatory submission. Treating it as a standalone priority-3 item would create unnecessary overhead. Q1 action: confirm AES-256 key scheduling is within the implant MCU power and latency budget and flag it as a committed inclusion in the combined firmware update specification.
